title: Monetary policy transmission: why consumers' housing situations matter
summary: The ECB Blog examines how monetary policy transmission to households differs based on consumers' housing and mortgage situations across euro area countries. Using data from the ECB's Consumer Expectations Survey (CES), the analysis finds that real interest rate expectations for mortgages rose noticeably since October 2022, and that countries with larger shares of adjustable-rate mortgages (Portugal, Spain, Finland) experienced faster policy transmission than those with predominantly fixed-rate mortgages (Netherlands, Germany). The research also identifies a positive correlation between consumers' expected interest rates and expected inflation, which is strongest for adjustable-rate mortgage holders.
key_points: Real interest rate expectations for mortgages rose noticeably since October 2022, reflecting the start of the ECB rate hiking cycle in July 2022

Monetary policy transmission varies significantly across euro area countries based on mortgage type: countries like Portugal, Spain, and Finland (more adjustable-rate mortgages) have faster transmission; Netherlands and Germany (more fixed-rate mortgages) have slower transmission
Homeowners without mortgages (more common in Italy) are not directly affected by interest rate changes but may be indirectly affected through wealth effects if house prices decline
Renters (more prevalent in Germany) are not directly affected by mortgage rate changes but may face indirect effects if landlords raise rents
Consumers who expect higher interest rates also tend to expect higher inflation over the next twelve months, suggesting they don't expect tightening to reduce inflation in the short term
The positive relationship between nominal interest rate expectations and inflation expectations is strongest for consumers holding adjustable-rate mortgages
why_it_matters: This research is relevant for ECB policymakers as it highlights that monetary policy affects households unevenly depending on their housing situation. Countries with higher shares of adjustable-rate mortgages may experience faster and stronger transmission of rate decisions to consumer behavior, meaning the same ECB policy decision could have different real-economy effects across euro area members. For investors, this suggests that housing market dynamics and consumer spending responses to ECB policy may vary geographically.
macro_context: This blog post provides empirical support for heterogeneous monetary policy transmission across the euro area based on housing market structures. The finding that adjustable-rate mortgage holders show the strongest link between interest rate expectations and inflation expectations suggests that monetary policy tightening may have a more direct pass-through to perceived cost-of-living pressures in countries like Portugal and Spain. This could have implications for inflation persistence and wage demands in those economies.
important_numbers: 20,000 consumers surveyed monthly in the ECB Consumer Expectations Survey (CES)
October 2022 marked the start of noticeable rise in real mortgage interest rate expectations
July 2022 marked the start of the ECB rate hiking cycle

issues_found: Source published October 3, 2024 (approximately 18 months before TODAY); however, this is a substantive ECB staff research commentary on historical monetary policy transmission patterns with clear archival value
Source body was truncated at [TRUNCATED]; however, the key findings on heterogeneity of transmission across euro area mortgage types appear to be captured in the key_points

notes: This is a historical research commentary from the ECB Blog (published October 2024). While it is substantive and uses the ECB's own Consumer Expectations Survey data, the publication date means the specific survey data points cited (up to June 2024) are now historical. The source does not discuss market structure (market plumbing, operations, or infrastructure) and is therefore not relevant to that topic. Market impact confidence is set to 0 because this is economic research commentary without direct market-moving implications. No asset classes are listed as the source does not make directional market predictions.
